documents.name: DUCAR.docx
summary_points: X X +1 Synonyms and Related Terms District, Urban and Community Access Roads Local Government Roads Rural Roads Network DUCAR Maintenance Programme Key Data Points Total Size: Approximately 138,510 km.

The DUCAR network refers to the District, Urban, and Community Access Roads network in Uganda, representing the largest share of the country’s road infrastructure, accounting for approximately 86.8% (138,510 km) of the total road network.
Key Aspects of the DUCAR Network Structure: Comprised of district roads (~38,603 km), urban roads (~17,849 km), and community access roads (~79,948 km).
Condition: More than 40% (up to 60%) is considered to be beyond maintenance level, requiring rehabilitation.

table_extracts.title: DUCAR_APA_References_and_Assumptions_Register / Decision_Assumptions
rows: Use a road-and-bridge framework rather than pavement-only prioritisation.
DUCAR includes bridges, culverts, drainage and access constraints; PIARC and FHWA frame road asset management as whole-road-infrastructure business processes, not a single pavement tool.
Keep the tool usable offline in Excel and standalone HTML.

DUCAR use will include local governments with uneven connectivity and varied software maturity; India and South Africa rural road systems show the value of simple maintainable systems with GIS/data paths.
South Africa RRAMS; India RMMS; PIARC
A failed asset should not consume routine maintenance funds merely because it scores badly. This follows lifecycle and maintainability logic from asset management practice.
Use maintainability as a hard classification field.
Separating maintainable works from rehabilitation/capital referrals protects routine budgets and makes backlog transparent.
India RMMS; FHWA; UNRA RAM policy
Use critical access, not only traffic volume.
DUCAR roads serve schools, health facilities, markets and administrative access where traffic counts may understate social value.
India PMGSY; NZ ONF; PIARC critical assets
Ring-fence bridge and major culvert risk visibly.
Bridge risk can be masked by road-length-based ranking; Japan and FHWA both emphasise bridge inspection and bridge condition in asset management.
Japan MLIT; FHWA; UNRA BMS manuals
Make GIS export a core output.
South Africa RRAMS/GeoRAMS and New Zealand ONF show the institutional value of spatially classifying and communicating network investment decisions.
South Africa RRAMS; NZ ONF; PIARC

table_extracts.title: DUCAR_APA_References_and_Assumptions_Register / Country_Case_Studies
rows: Rural Roads Asset Management System grant and GeoRAMS GIS/data platform for district municipal roads.
DUCAR should combine condition data, traffic data, GIS storage and district capacity building, not only produce annual spreadsheets.
Use district-level templates, GIS-ready outputs and a controlled data dictionary that can later migrate into an enterprise RAMS.
Existing UNRA road infrastructure asset management policy, RMS/PMS/BMS manuals, visual assessment forms, traffic/QMP material and bridge/culvert inventory dictionaries.
The DUCAR tool should not invent a parallel universe; it should reuse UNRA field concepts, BMS fields, performance indicators and QA practice where appropriate.
Map DUCAR fields to Road_ID, Bridge_ID, condition, drainage, traffic proxy, intervention, unit rate, maintainability and GIS join keys.
PMGSY rural roads connectivity and maintenance management guidance supported by national technical and management standards.
Rural road programmes need simple asset registers, maintainability logic, dedicated maintenance planning and district-level ownership.
DUCAR should maintain simple inventories, enforce routine-vs-capital separation and publish district-level selected/deferred lists.
Periodic inspection guidelines, technical assistance teams and national support for municipalities facing complex bridge/road maintenance.
Bridge and structure risk requires regular inspection, escalation and technical backstopping where local capacity is limited.
Use bridge ring-fence, referral flags, technical review gates and training modules for severe bridge/culvert findings.
Well-managed Highway Infrastructure Code of Practice shifts local authorities toward risk-based local levels of service.
A framework can be nationally consistent while allowing local risk, affordability and service-level judgement.
DUCAR weights should have national defaults but allow approved local calibration and documented overrides.
FHWA risk-based TAMPs integrate pavement and bridge asset summaries, condition, lifecycle cost, risk, financial plans and investment strategies.
Annual programming should connect condition, risk and finance rather than presenting a list of projects without funding logic.
DUCAR programme outputs include selected cost, deferred maintenance backlog, referral backlog and budget scenarios.
Road asset management advances framed through the PIARC Asset Management Manual.
PIARC maturity framing is useful for countries building asset management capability incrementally.
DUCAR should start with minimum viable data and progressively mature toward lifecycle, performance and enterprise databases.

documents.name: DUCAR_Maintenance_Allocation_and_Prioritisation_Framework.docx
summary_points: DUCAR Maintenance Allocation and Prioritisation Framework Straightforward technical framework, decision rules, data model, governance system and Excel-based decision-support tool documentation Prepared as a working consultant draft | DUCAR network | 2026-05-05 1.

Purpose: turn DUCAR road and bridge condition data, budget ceilings, unit rates and policy rules into a transparent annual maintenance programme that can be defended technically, audited financially, joined to GIS, and repeated each year by national and local government staff.
Use enhanced tools where available: Survey123 forms, GPS-enabled tablets, Roadroid roughness, ROMDAS/visual inspection outputs, drainage measurement tools, bridge inspection forms and GIS basemaps.
The framework supports future integration with ArcGIS, Survey123, ROMDAS, Roadroid, PMS/BMS, HDM-4 and RED, but does not require expensive data to begin annual prioritisation.

documents.name: DUCAR_Maintenance_Allocation_and_Prioritisation_Policy.docx
summary_points: DUCAR Maintenance Allocation and Prioritisation Policy Policy draft for institutional adoption and annual DUCAR programme governance DUCAR network | Working implementation pack | 2026-05-05 1.
Maintenance Boundary Routine maintenance budgets shall not be used to hide rehabilitation or capital-upgrading needs, except for explicitly approved emergency safety/access actions.
Policy Clauses Allocation Principles The programme shall balance preventive maintenance, critical access, bridge safety, climate/drainage vulnerability, equity and readiness.
Bridge Ring-Fence Bridge and major culvert risks shall be visible and may be ring-fenced so they are not lost in road-length-based allocation.

summary_points: It is furthermore expected that after the publishing of the ISO 55000 standard in 2014 that it will become a mechanism and benchmark for asset rich organisations (and therefore also roads authorities) to show that they practice good road infrastructure asset management.
The current road network of 21,000km for which UNRA is responsible, will not increase substantially without a corresponding increase in Road Fund allocations to UNRA for maintenance commitments.

The adoption of the ISO 55000 international standard will require that UNRA accept a Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy (RIAMP) (in process), and prepare a Strategic Asset Management Plan (SAMP) (not yet prepared).
In terms of earlier discussions around ISO 55000, they cover assets and asset systems but lack a focus on the asset management system per se.

tabularExtracts.title: DUCAR_APA_References_and_Assumptions_Register / Country_Case_Studies
rows: Rural Roads Asset Management System grant and GeoRAMS GIS/data platform for district municipal roads.
DUCAR should combine condition data, traffic data, GIS storage and district capacity building, not only produce annual spreadsheets.
Use district-level templates, GIS-ready outputs and a controlled data dictionary that can later migrate into an enterprise RAMS.
Existing UNRA road infrastructure asset management policy, RMS/PMS/BMS manuals, visual assessment forms, traffic/QMP material and bridge/culvert inventory dictionaries.
The DUCAR tool should not invent a parallel universe; it should reuse UNRA field concepts, BMS fields, performance indicators and QA practice where appropriate.
Map DUCAR fields to Road_ID, Bridge_ID, condition, drainage, traffic proxy, intervention, unit rate, maintainability and GIS join keys.
PMGSY rural roads connectivity and maintenance management guidance supported by national technical and management standards.
Rural road programmes need simple asset registers, maintainability logic, dedicated maintenance planning and district-level ownership.
DUCAR should maintain simple inventories, enforce routine-vs-capital separation and publish district-level selected/deferred lists.
Periodic inspection guidelines, technical assistance teams and national support for municipalities facing complex bridge/road maintenance.
Bridge and structure risk requires regular inspection, escalation and technical backstopping where local capacity is limited.
Use bridge ring-fence, referral flags, technical review gates and training modules for severe bridge/culvert findings.
Well-managed Highway Infrastructure Code of Practice shifts local authorities toward risk-based local levels of service.
A framework can be nationally consistent while allowing local risk, affordability and service-level judgement.
DUCAR weights should have national defaults but allow approved local calibration and documented overrides.
FHWA risk-based TAMPs integrate pavement and bridge asset summaries, condition, lifecycle cost, risk, financial plans and investment strategies.
Annual programming should connect condition, risk and finance rather than presenting a list of projects without funding logic.
DUCAR programme outputs include selected cost, deferred maintenance backlog, referral backlog and budget scenarios.
Road asset management advances framed through the PIARC Asset Management Manual.
PIARC maturity framing is useful for countries building asset management capability incrementally.
DUCAR should start with minimum viable data and progressively mature toward lifecycle, performance and enterprise databases.

tabularExtracts.title: DUCAR_APA_References_and_Assumptions_Register / Decision_Assumptions
rows: Use a road-and-bridge framework rather than pavement-only prioritisation.
DUCAR includes bridges, culverts, drainage and access constraints; PIARC and FHWA frame road asset management as whole-road-infrastructure business processes, not a single pavement tool.
Keep the tool usable offline in Excel and standalone HTML.

DUCAR use will include local governments with uneven connectivity and varied software maturity; India and South Africa rural road systems show the value of simple maintainable systems with GIS/data paths.
South Africa RRAMS; India RMMS; PIARC
A failed asset should not consume routine maintenance funds merely because it scores badly. This follows lifecycle and maintainability logic from asset management practice.
Use maintainability as a hard classification field.
Separating maintainable works from rehabilitation/capital referrals protects routine budgets and makes backlog transparent.
India RMMS; FHWA; UNRA RAM policy
Use critical access, not only traffic volume.
DUCAR roads serve schools, health facilities, markets and administrative access where traffic counts may understate social value.
India PMGSY; NZ ONF; PIARC critical assets
Ring-fence bridge and major culvert risk visibly.
Bridge risk can be masked by road-length-based ranking; Japan and FHWA both emphasise bridge inspection and bridge condition in asset management.
Japan MLIT; FHWA; UNRA BMS manuals
Make GIS export a core output.
South Africa RRAMS/GeoRAMS and New Zealand ONF show the institutional value of spatially classifying and communicating network investment decisions.
South Africa RRAMS; NZ ONF; PIARC

decisionAssumptions.title: Global case package: Decision Assumptions
rows: Use a road-and-bridge framework rather than pavement-only prioritisation.
DUCAR includes bridges, culverts, drainage and access constraints; PIARC and FHWA frame road asset management as whole-road-infrastructure business processes, not a single pavement tool.
Keep the tool usable offline in Excel and standalone HTML.

DUCAR use will include local governments with uneven connectivity and varied software maturity; India and South Africa rural road systems show the value of simple maintainable systems with GIS/data paths.
South Africa RRAMS; India RMMS; PIARC
A failed asset should not consume routine maintenance funds merely because it scores badly. This follows lifecycle and maintainability logic from asset management practice.
Use maintainability as a hard classification field.
Separating maintainable works from rehabilitation/capital referrals protects routine budgets and makes backlog transparent.
India RMMS; FHWA; UNRA RAM policy
Use critical access, not only traffic volume.
DUCAR roads serve schools, health facilities, markets and administrative access where traffic counts may understate social value.
India PMGSY; NZ ONF; PIARC critical assets
Ring-fence bridge and major culvert risk visibly.
Bridge risk can be masked by road-length-based ranking; Japan and FHWA both emphasise bridge inspection and bridge condition in asset management.
Japan MLIT; FHWA; UNRA BMS manuals
Make GIS export a core output.
South Africa RRAMS/GeoRAMS and New Zealand ONF show the institutional value of spatially classifying and communicating network investment decisions.
South Africa RRAMS; NZ ONF; PIARC
Use APA-style source traceability for assumptions.
The framework must be defensible in procurement, audit and institutional adoption settings.
TAMP practice requires financial plans and investment strategies; scenario outputs convert technical ranking into funding dialogue.
Build a training and handover layer.
PIARC and Japan MLIT both treat organisational capacity, training and technical support as part of implementation, not optional extras.
PIARC; Japan MLIT; UNRA user manuals

countryCaseStudies.title: Global case package: Country Case Studies
rows: Rural Roads Asset Management System grant and GeoRAMS GIS/data platform for district municipal roads.
DUCAR should combine condition data, traffic data, GIS storage and district capacity building, not only produce annual spreadsheets.
Use district-level templates, GIS-ready outputs and a controlled data dictionary that can later migrate into an enterprise RAMS.
Existing UNRA road infrastructure asset management policy, RMS/PMS/BMS manuals, visual assessment forms, traffic/QMP material and bridge/culvert inventory dictionaries.
The DUCAR tool should not invent a parallel universe; it should reuse UNRA field concepts, BMS fields, performance indicators and QA practice where appropriate.
Map DUCAR fields to Road_ID, Bridge_ID, condition, drainage, traffic proxy, intervention, unit rate, maintainability and GIS join keys.
PMGSY rural roads connectivity and maintenance management guidance supported by national technical and management standards.
Rural road programmes need simple asset registers, maintainability logic, dedicated maintenance planning and district-level ownership.
DUCAR should maintain simple inventories, enforce routine-vs-capital separation and publish district-level selected/deferred lists.
Periodic inspection guidelines, technical assistance teams and national support for municipalities facing complex bridge/road maintenance.
Bridge and structure risk requires regular inspection, escalation and technical backstopping where local capacity is limited.
Use bridge ring-fence, referral flags, technical review gates and training modules for severe bridge/culvert findings.
Well-managed Highway Infrastructure Code of Practice shifts local authorities toward risk-based local levels of service.
A framework can be nationally consistent while allowing local risk, affordability and service-level judgement.
DUCAR weights should have national defaults but allow approved local calibration and documented overrides.
FHWA risk-based TAMPs integrate pavement and bridge asset summaries, condition, lifecycle cost, risk, financial plans and investment strategies.
Annual programming should connect condition, risk and finance rather than presenting a list of projects without funding logic.
DUCAR programme outputs include selected cost, deferred maintenance backlog, referral backlog and budget scenarios.
Road asset management advances framed through the PIARC Asset Management Manual.
PIARC maturity framing is useful for countries building asset management capability incrementally.
DUCAR should start with minimum viable data and progressively mature toward lifecycle, performance and enterprise databases.
Austroads Guide to Asset Management applies ISO 55000 principles to road networks, condition monitoring, prediction and risk practice.
Asset management should be whole-of-organisation and performance/risk-based.
DUCAR governance should cover roles, policy, data, tools, training, audit and continuous improvement.
One Network Framework links movement, place, levels of service and future planning to investment decisions.
Road function and place/access value should shape investment decisions, not traffic alone.
DUCAR critical access scoring should include schools, health facilities, markets, administrative access and emergency routes.
